title: What an American thinks about Macedonia

transcript.text: Okay, what an American thinks about Macedonia.
I went back into Skopje, the capital of Macedonia, from Pristina, the capital of Kosovo, in one day.
It was only 89 kilometers, and Skopje is right on the north part.
I got a flat on my way in, but then I changed tires, and then I kind of walked my bike over these tired people who filled it up really hard for free.
They were cool.
And then I rode into town, and I got this party, this hostel.
It was a hostel called, like, really close to the center, like, five-minute bike ride from the center, called Art Hostel.
And it was actually fun because they had all these signs.
I originally wanted to go to, like, Hostel Hostel for some reason because I thought it was the cheapest one, but then I went to Hostel.
It was not even cheap.
The girl charged me, like, 10 euros, and she was really hot, too, and really, like, pretty and, like, really, like, laughing all the time, man.
She laughed all the time.
She's doing things, she, like, laughed.
And then I noticed other Macedonians do that, too.
So Macedonians is, like, a country where they laugh.
They say something, and then they laugh.
Like, in Serbia, I forgot to say, like, when they talk, it sounds like they're, like, yelling and stuff.
But, like, it's, I guess Macedonians can sometimes talk like that, too, but, but, like, yeah, they talk and then laugh, like, and they're really friendly, too.
Like, really, like, happy to have a tourist there, you know, and to help you out, and smiling at you, and, like, really, they're really friendly people, and it was a really cheap place.
And it seemed, you know, like, safe, you know, and, um, Scopias are pretty.
